data.title: 3 Major Questions For Investors In March
data.description:
Equity markets have held up much better than expected this week given the opening up of a significant regional conflict in Iran, which has caused energy prices to spike. Well, at least U.S. equities have weathered the storm well. Asia stock markets have been hit with considerably more volatility in this week's trading sessions.

The conflict in Iran is just the latest key worry for investors that has emerged in recent months. U.S. equities have remained quite resilient in recent weeks despite volatility rising and with the market trading at historically high valuations. However, these events have spurred major new questions for investors. And how these three key issues get resolved in the coming weeks and months could determine the direction of the markets.

#1 - What Is The End Game In Iran?

This is the question that will likely dominate the headlines in the coming days and weeks. To me, regime change in Iran seems a very unlikely outcome, outside a huge number of 'boots on the ground', something the administration has stated it would avoid. No significant regime change in history has been accomplished via air power alone.

A civil war like those recently in Libya or Syria is always a possibility over the longer term. But little appears to be happening on the ground in Iran to point to that scenario. If a civil war took place in Iran, there could be a migrant inflow into Europe that would make the Syrian migration wave of the mid-2010s look like a walk in the park. Iran has some 90 million people, and the country is roughly half the size of Europe on a land mass basis.

While using $4 million Patriot missiles to shoot down $25,000 drones and $250,000 to $500,000 ballistic missiles is a great business model for Raytheon (RTX), it is not a cost-effective way to fight a war. More importantly, it is not sustainable given the limited number of interceptors in the U.S. arsenal and needs in other regions (Ukraine).

Therefore, the most likely scenario in my view is some sort of negotiated end to this conflict will happen several weeks down the line, perhaps along the lines of what recently happened in Venezuela. With enough concessions to allow both sides to save face after Iranian military assets have been degraded further and key munitions start to run out on the U.S./Israel side.

However, events on the ground could always change that calculus. In the meantime, markets will continue to be hit with volatility from news on the ground. The longer this conflict drags on the more upward pressure it will put on oil prices. This will be a significant tailwind for inflation, which in turn reduces the chances of further rate cuts from the Federal Reserve until resolved.

The impacts to the rest of the world will be higher from Operation Epic Fury, which has curtailed most of the crude exports out of the Middle East as traffic has largely ceased flowing through the Strait of Hormuz, where some 20% of global crude shipments go through. This region is particularly a huge source of energy supplies to Asia. Roughly 80% (1.4 million bpd) of Iranian crude exports go to China it should be noted. A key reason that stock exchanges in this region like the Kospi have been so volatile over the past week.

Interestingly, Kharg Island which is located off the country's southern coast in the northern Persian Gulf, has not been hit in this conflict. 80% to 90% of Iranian oil exports flow out of this massive supertanker loading facility. Taking Kharg Island offline would cripple the Iranian regime financially. It would also trigger a huge spike in oil prices and would also likely result in retaliatory strikes on energy facilities in Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states.

#2 - How Many More 'Cockroaches' Will Emerge In The Credit Markets?'

Before news from Iran overtook headlines, the deterioration in parts of the credit markets were one of the two main worries for investors in February. Events at Blue Owl Capital (OWL) triggered new worries around potential new 'cockroaches' in the burgeoning private credit markets. These worries had largely remained dormant since the immediate aftermath of the unexpected bankruptcies of First Brands and Tricolor Holdings late last summer.

Last week, a UK mortgage provider called Market Financial Solutions collapsed. This week, Blackstone (BX) announced it had record redemption requests (7.9% of AUM) in its $82 billion private credit fund. Jamie Dimon recently warned that the leader of JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) sees some similarities to the lead up to the Great Financial Crisis present in today's credit markets. Other prominent financial leaders like Lloyd Blankfein have been issuing warnings on the private credit markets recently as well. So, will significant new problems emerge from credit markets in the weeks and months ahead? Prudent investors should be monitoring these developments closely.

#3 - Are Fears Of AI Disruption Overblown?

The other big issue in February was growing fears around potential AI disruption. The now infamous 'SaaSpocalypse' opened the month and pressured many parts of the software sector throughout February. These fears also hit other industries sporadically. The stocks of insurance brokers, commercial real estate services, wealth management concerns, and logistical/trucking firms all took their turn in the barrel during the month.

Then late last week, Block (XYZ) disclosed it was laying off approximately 4,000 employees, roughly 40% of its entire workforce. The company's CEO stated this action was taken due to improving AI capabilities that made these functions redundant. This ignited new fears that AI will disrupt the U.S. jobs markets faster than previously forecasted and XYZ was rewarded by a 17% rally in its stock following the reduction in force notice. Wednesday's better than expected February ADP jobs report was a relief, although job openings remain at a five-year low.

Will March bring new layoff announcements akin to Block's and new fears AI will upend entire industries? Definitely something investors should monitor. The flip side to this question, is if AI doesn't prove to be massively disruptive, how will hyperscalers and firms like OpenAI (OPENAI) earn an acceptable return on their massive AI investments?

I continue to be cautious on the overall market. Equities feel like they are priced close to perfection within a market backdrop that is far less than ideal. Therefore, my model portfolio continues to consist of roughly a 75% allocation to covered call holdings around the few stocks in this market that sport reasonable valuations. Most of the rest of my portfolio is in short-term treasuries and cash, awaiting lower entry points.

data.title: Touchstone Small Company Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Highlights

  • Invests primarily in common stocks of small companies
  • Research intensive approach focuses on finding high quality companies that are believed to have improving business models, solid management teams, sustainable growth potential and favorable industry dynamics
  • Quality companies are those that possess the following traits: consistent earnings, reasonable valuations, low debt levels, solid free cash flows and strong management with a history of good capital allocation

Market Recap

Volatility during the quarter was significantly lower than the first three quarters of the year, which witnessed the Russell 2000 Index experience a bear market decline of 22% from the start of the year to the April 8 lows. Then, the Russell 2000 rallied 50% to the mid-December high. We believe the small cap rally was driven by relief that tariff rates were lowered and did not impact the economy and corporate earnings as originally feared; the “One Big Beautiful Bill” provided fiscal stimulus and removed policy uncertainty, and the U.S. Federal Reserve provided monetary stimulus through lower interest rate targets and expectations. All of this provided a more favorable backdrop for small cap earnings.

The benchmark sectors that outperformed during the quarter were Health Care, Communication Services, Materials, and Real Estate. The benchmark sectors that underperformed for the quarter were Consumer Staples, Information Technology (IT), Consumer Discretionary, Industrials, Utilities, Financials, and Energy.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Small Company Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived)(SAGWX) outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Index, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025.

The Fund’s top three performing sectors in terms of adding value were IT and Industrials, both driven by stock selection, and Consumer Staples, due to an underweight allocation. The Fund’s bottom three performing sectors that detracted from relative performance were Health Care, Consumer Discretionary, and Materials, due primarily to weak stock performance.

Performance was driven by strong gains across Health Care and IT holdings. Haemonetics Corp. (HAE)(Health Care sector) rebounded sharply following better-than-expected growth in its plasma business, validating our decision to add on prior weakness given durable growth catalysts. Tower Semiconductor Ltd. (TSEM) and Ciena Corp. (CIEN)(both IT sector) also posted outsized returns as accelerating artificial intelligence infrastructure investment fueled demand for silicon photonics and optical networking equipment. Additional contributions came from Globus Medical Inc. (GMED)(Health Care sector), where operational improvements led to stronger earnings growth, and Progyny Inc. (PGNY)(Health Care sector), which benefited from improving utilization trends and strong client additions that enhanced long-term growth visibility.

Detractors were largely driven by company-specific execution issues and valuation resets. Stride Inc. (LRN)(Consumer Discretionary sector) declined following disruptions tied to a software platform transition that impacted enrollment growth, though the position is being closely monitored. Doximity Inc. (DOCS) and Certara Inc. (CERT)(both Health Care sector) sold off amid investor concerns over biopharma marketing and spending visibility in 2026, prompting us to add at more attractive valuations given intact fundamentals. Commvault Systems Inc. (CVLT)(IT sector) declined as reported decelerating subscription growth led to valuation compression despite continued strength in underlying recurring revenue. Grand Canyon Education Inc. (LOPE)(Consumer Discretionary sector) also declined amid broader weakness in education services stocks, despite reporting strong results, and was added on weakness.

During the quarter, the Fund initiated positions in Franklin Electric Co. Inc. (FELE) (Industrials sector), LCI Industries Inc. (LCII)(Consumer Discretionary sector), Q2 Holdings Inc. (QTWO)(IT sector), Tidewater Inc. (TDW)(Energy sector), Waystar Holding Corp. (WAY)(Health Care sector), and Zeta Global Holdings Corp. (ZETA)(IT sector), reflecting opportunities across various sectors. The portfolio exited CSG Systems International Inc. (CSGS)(IT sector) following the announcement of its acquisition.

Outlook and Conclusion

Small cap and large cap 2026 earnings are both expected to increase 15%. Small cap relative valuations to large cap hit a 25-year low in mid-November, and while modestly off the low remain at a 30% discount. We have been writing for three years the small cap vs. large cap performance was driven primarily by fundamentals, i.e., significantly better relative large cap earnings growth. With earnings growth now on par and with discounted valuation, the small cap risk/reward is the most favorable it has been in several years.

Small cap price returns in 2025 were driven by non-earners (+31%); low quality (lowest quintile ROE +25%); and high beta (top quintile +20%). This happens in market environments like we experienced from the April lows. We believe that the fourth quarter market environment could reflect a return to fundamentals driving equity returns. In our view, that type of market environment is favorable to the Fund, which invests only in profitable small cap stocks with above average quality and below average volatility. We continue to execute our investment process consistently, identify compelling new investments, manage risk, and target the consistent risk/return profile the Fund has delivered since inception.

A Word About Risk

The Fund invests in equities which are subject to market volatility and loss. The Fund invests in stocks of small-cap companies, which may be subject to more erratic market movements than stocks of larger, more established companies. The Fund invests in foreign securities which carry the associated risks of economic and political instability, market liquidity, currency volatility and accounting standards that differ from those of U.S. markets and may offer less protection to investors. The Fund may focus its investments in a particular industry and/or market sector which may increase the Fund’s volatility and magnify its effects on total return. Events in the U.S. and global financial markets, including actions taken to stimulate or stabilize economic growth may at times result in unusually high market volatility, which could negatively impact Fund performance and cause it to experience illiquidity, shareholder redemptions, or other potentially adverse effects. Banks and financial services companies could suffer losses if interest rates rise or economic conditions deteriorate. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to change. The Advisor engages a sub- advisor to make investment decisions for the Fund’s portfolio; it may be unable to identify and retain a sub-advisor who achieves superior investment returns relative to other similar sub-advisors.

Please consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund carefully before investing. The prospectus and the summary prospectus contain this and other information about the Fund. To obtain a prospectus or a summary prospectus, contact your financial professional or download and/or request one at TouchstoneInvestments.com/resources or call Touchstone at 800.638.8194.
